sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 4-year-old mother of twins, presented with a complaint of a painful right knee that she injured while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She caught her ski and fell, resulting in the knee popping and causing ongoing pain since then.
She was unable to bear weight and had to be taken down by the ski patrol.
The pain is located on the outside of the knee and also feels like it won't hold her weight.
She has been unable to bend the knee since the injury, but can straighten it partially.
She has not experienced any numbness or tingling in her toes.
She has been taking ibuprofen for the pain, which has been somewhat helpful.
Past medical history: The patient reported being otherwise generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other significant medical conditions.
She denied any allergies to medications.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms in other organs were reported.
Current medications: Ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient had a regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and no murmurs on heart exam.
The right knee examination revealed edema and an effusion, with pain to palpation on the lateral aspect.
There was decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, and palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses.
No leg edema in the ankle was noted.
Test Results: The patient had a normal X-ray of the right knee, which did not show any bony abnormalities.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in the right knee.
Problem list: ACL tear; right knee pain; decreased range of motion in the right knee; knee effusion.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis and assess for any other potential injuries.
I also recommended a knee brace to provide support to the knee.
Follow-up appointment will be scheduled to discuss the MRI results and plan further management.
Follow-up: The patient will return in a couple of days to discuss the MRI results and plan further management.
